summary: Validated record with machine-checkable proof for the official NTFS encryption policy surface. The app now writes only the documented policy path.

setting.name: Disable NTFS Encryption (EFS)
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can encrypt files on NTFS volumes by using EFS. This policy decides whether that encryption feature is allowed on NTFS volumes.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable it when they want to prevent accidental encrypted files or avoid support issues caused by lost EFS access.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off can simplify file recovery and support, but it also removes a built-in file-encryption option.
setting.professional_notes: FileSys.admx documents the official policy at HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Policies\NtfsDisableEncryption with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The app now writes that official value only.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force the NTFS encryption policy through Group Policy.

allowed_values.label: NTFS encryption allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and NTFS encryption is not blocked by this policy.

allowed_values.label: NTFS encryption disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Encryption is not allowed on NTFS volumes through the documented policy setting.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes the documented NTFS encryption policy value under System\CurrentControlSet\Policies only.

windows_defaults.label: Windows policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems supporting the NTFS encryption policy

states.rationale: Leave the official NTFS encryption policy unset.

windows_defaults.notes: The local policy text says a reboot is required for this setting to take effect.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows managed default
intended_for: Systems that may still use EFS
avoid_for: Machines that explicitly want EFS blocked

states.rationale: Leave the official policy unset until the extra runtime write is resolved.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to avoid publishing an implementation that mixes a documented policy with an undocumented extra write.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a finalized published EFS-off preset immediately.
tradeoffs: EFS remains under the normal Windows baseline.
The current app write remains documented but not validated.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft FileSys.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps the NTFS encryption policy to HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Policies\NtfsDisableEncryption with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft FileSys.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the setting prevents access to and creation of encrypted files and requires a reboot to take effect.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes NtfsDisableEncryption = 1 under System\CurrentControlSet\Policies.

decision.why: The official EFS policy surface is now validated and the app writes only the documented policy path. The setting remains high-impact and is not recommended for general users because it disables EFS on NTFS volumes.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: C:\Windows\PolicyDefinitions\FileSys.admx (NtfsDisableEncryption enabledValue=1, disabledValue=0); C:\Windows\PolicyDefinitions\en-US\FileSys.adml (enabling the setting prevents access to and creation of encrypted files and requires a reboot to take effect).
validation_proof.notes: Validated against the local Microsoft policy files for NTFS encryption.
